frameworks.name: normative Bayesian inference
frameworks.one_line: Unified Bayesian model adopts the appropriate generative model per environment

frameworks.name: change-point dynamics
frameworks.one_line: Abrupt nonstationarity producing discrete jumps in the latent mean

frameworks.name: random-walk dynamics
frameworks.one_line: Gradual drift nonstationarity producing slow continuous change

frameworks.name: adaptive learning rate
frameworks.one_line: Prediction-error sensitivity is modulated by inferred environmental structure

core_question: Do humans adapt their learning to reflect the generative structure (change-point vs random-walk) of their environment?
key_assumptions: Prediction errors are interpreted in light of an inferred generative model
Mean and variance are jointly inferred latent variables
A unified Bayesian framework can capture both environments
manipulations: Environment type: change-point versus random-walk dynamics
Magnitude of prediction errors (small vs large)
Sources of variability: changes in mean versus changes in variance
key_findings: Humans adapted learning normatively between change-point and random-walk environments
Identical prediction errors were interpreted differently across the two environments
Large prediction errors sharply increased learning rate under change-points but not random walks
Explicit uncertainty reports tracked variance and showed sustained shifts only after variance change points
interpretations: Humans dissociate mean and variance (stochasticity) as separately inferred latent variables
Adaptive learning reflects the generative structure of environmental dynamics
